stops.title: Thales and Natural Causes
stops.subtitle: What if the universe runs on rules, not gods?
stops.description: Thales was the first to claim that natural events have natural explanations. This single idea is the foundation of all science.

stops.title: Pythagoras and Mathematical Harmony
stops.subtitle: Music, numbers, and the shape of the cosmos
stops.description: Pythagoras discovered that musical harmony follows precise mathematical ratios — and concluded that mathematics is the deep structure of reality.

stops.title: Democritus and the Atom
stops.subtitle: Cut matter in half. Keep cutting. Where does it stop?
stops.description: Democritus proposed that all matter is made of indivisible particles — atoms — moving through empty void. He was right, 2,300 years before proof.

stops.title: Aristotle's Theory of Motion
stops.subtitle: Why heavy things fall faster (they don't)
stops.description: Aristotle believed heavier objects fall faster than lighter ones. This simulation lets you test his prediction against reality.

stops.title: Archimedes and Buoyancy
stops.subtitle: Eureka! Why things float
stops.description: Drag objects of different densities into water. The displaced volume equals the buoyant force — Archimedes discovered this in his bath.

stops.title: Eratosthenes Measures the Earth
stops.subtitle: A stick, a shadow, and the size of a world
stops.description: Eratosthenes calculated the Earth's circumference using two sticks and their shadows on the same day. He was accurate to within 2%.

stops.title: Ptolemy's Geocentric Model
stops.subtitle: A wrong theory that predicted the sky perfectly
stops.description: Ptolemy's Earth-centered model with epicycles predicted planetary positions accurately for 1,400 years — and was completely wrong.

stops.title: Copernicus and the Heliocentric Model
stops.subtitle: What if the Earth moves?
stops.description: Copernicus proposed the Sun, not the Earth, sits at the center. Toggle between models and watch how the planetary paths simplify.

stops.title: Galileo's Inclined Plane
stops.subtitle: How to measure gravity without a stopwatch
stops.description: Galileo slowed gravity down with an inclined plane to prove that distance grows as the square of time — regardless of the object's mass.

stops.title: Galileo's Pendulum
stops.subtitle: The clock hiding in a chandelier
stops.description: Galileo noticed the cathedral chandelier swinging — and timed it with his own pulse. Change the mass; the period stays the same. Change the length; it changes.

stops.title: Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion
stops.subtitle: Orbits are ellipses. Time moves unequally.
stops.description: Kepler discovered that planets trace ellipses, sweep equal areas in equal times, and that orbital periods follow a precise mathematical law.

stops.title: Newton's Laws of Motion
stops.subtitle: F = ma. Three laws that governed physics for 200 years.
stops.description: Apply forces to objects. Watch inertia, acceleration, and reaction play out exactly as Newton's three laws predict.

stops.title: Newton's Universal Gravitation
stops.subtitle: The same force that drops an apple holds the Moon in orbit
stops.description: Newton realized that falling apples and orbiting moons obey the same inverse-square law. Fire a cannon fast enough — it orbits instead of landing.

stops.title: Hooke's Law and Elasticity
stops.subtitle: Springs, stress, and why things bounce back
stops.description: Hooke discovered that extension is proportional to force — the simplest law of elasticity, and the basis of every spring and suspension system.

stops.title: Bernoulli's Principle
stops.subtitle: Why airplanes fly and curveballs curve
stops.description: As fluid speeds up, pressure drops. This single insight explains flight, carburetors, and the Magnus effect in spinning balls.

stops.title: Euler and Rigid Body Rotation
stops.subtitle: Why gyroscopes don't fall over
stops.description: Euler formalized the mathematics of rotating rigid bodies — the physics that keeps gyroscopes upright and explains precession.

stops.title: Coulomb's Law
stops.subtitle: Electricity follows an inverse square law — just like gravity
stops.description: Coulomb measured the force between electric charges and found it follows the same mathematical form as Newton's gravity — but can attract or repel.

stops.title: Volta and the Electric Battery
stops.subtitle: Storing electricity for the first time
stops.description: Volta stacked zinc and copper discs with wet cloth between them and produced a steady electric current — the first battery, enabling all of electrical science.

stops.title: Faraday's Electromagnetic Induction
stops.subtitle: Move a magnet through a coil — electricity appears
stops.description: Faraday — a bookbinder's apprentice with no formal education — discovered that a changing magnetic field creates an electric current. Every generator on Earth runs on this principle.

stops.title: Carnot and the Heat Engine
stops.subtitle: You can never convert all heat into work
stops.description: Carnot proved there is a fundamental limit to how efficiently any heat engine can run — a limit set by thermodynamics itself, not by engineering.

stops.title: Joule and Conservation of Energy
stops.subtitle: Heat is just motion in disguise
stops.description: Joule showed that mechanical work can be converted to heat with a precise ratio — establishing conservation of energy as a universal law.

stops.title: Maxwell's Equations
stops.subtitle: Four equations that contain all of classical electromagnetism
stops.description: Maxwell unified electricity, magnetism, and light into four elegant equations — and predicted that light itself is an electromagnetic wave.

stops.title: The Doppler Effect
stops.subtitle: Why an ambulance sounds different as it passes
stops.description: Moving sources compress or stretch the waves they emit. Doppler worked this out mathematically — it became the key to measuring the expansion of the universe.

stops.title: Boltzmann and Entropy
stops.subtitle: Why the universe always gets messier
stops.description: Boltzmann defined entropy as the number of ways a system can be arranged. His equation S = k log W links microscopic disorder to macroscopic thermodynamics.

stops.title: Hertz and Radio Waves
stops.subtitle: Maxwell's invisible light, detected at last
stops.description: Hertz built a spark-gap transmitter and detected the electromagnetic waves Maxwell had predicted — proving that invisible radiation travels at the speed of light.

stops.title: The Michelson-Morley Experiment
stops.subtitle: The most famous null result in physics
stops.description: They tried to measure Earth's motion through the luminiferous ether — and found nothing. This null result shattered classical physics and pointed toward Einstein.

stops.title: Planck and Blackbody Radiation
stops.subtitle: Energy comes in chunks — the birth of quantum theory
stops.description: Planck could only explain the glow of hot objects by assuming energy is quantized — emitted in discrete packets. He called it 'a desperate act.'

stops.title: Einstein and the Photoelectric Effect
stops.subtitle: Light is both a wave and a particle
stops.description: Einstein explained why shining light on metal ejects electrons — but only if the light frequency is high enough. This won him the Nobel Prize and proved light comes in photons.

stops.title: Special Relativity: Time Dilation
stops.subtitle: Moving clocks run slow
stops.description: Einstein showed that time passes more slowly for moving observers — and this is not an illusion. GPS satellites require relativity corrections to stay accurate.

stops.title: Special Relativity: Length Contraction
stops.subtitle: Moving objects are shorter
stops.description: Objects moving at high speed are measured as shorter along their direction of motion. Space itself compresses for fast-moving things.

stops.title: E = mc²
stops.subtitle: Mass and energy are the same thing
stops.description: Einstein's most famous equation says that mass is concentrated energy. A paperclip contains enough energy to power a city — if you could release it all.

stops.title: Rutherford and the Atomic Nucleus
stops.subtitle: The atom is mostly empty space
stops.description: Rutherford fired alpha particles at gold foil. Most passed straight through — but a few bounced directly back. The atom has a tiny, dense nucleus at its center.

stops.title: Bohr's Model of the Atom
stops.subtitle: Electrons jump between fixed energy levels
stops.description: Bohr proposed that electrons can only occupy specific energy levels — and emit light when they jump between them. Each element has a unique spectral fingerprint.

stops.title: General Relativity: Curved Spacetime
stops.subtitle: Gravity is the curvature of space and time
stops.description: Einstein replaced Newton's gravitational force with curved spacetime. Mass tells spacetime how to curve; curved spacetime tells mass how to move.

stops.title: Wave-Particle Duality
stops.subtitle: Light is a wave. Light is a particle. Both are true.
stops.description: De Broglie proposed that matter, like light, has wave properties. The double-slit experiment confirms it with eerie precision.

stops.title: Schrödinger's Wave Equation
stops.subtitle: The probability cloud that replaced the orbit
stops.description: Schrödinger's equation describes how quantum wavefunctions evolve in time — giving probabilities, not certainties, for where a particle will be found.

stops.title: Heisenberg's Uncertainty Principle
stops.subtitle: The universe has a fundamental limit on what you can know
stops.description: You cannot know a particle's position and momentum simultaneously with arbitrary precision. This is not a measurement problem — it is built into nature.

stops.title: The Pauli Exclusion Principle
stops.subtitle: Why matter is solid
stops.description: No two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state. This rule explains why matter resists compression and why the periodic table has the structure it does.

stops.title: Dirac and Antimatter
stops.subtitle: For every particle, there is an antiparticle
stops.description: Dirac's equation predicted that electrons must have mirror-image partners with opposite charge. The positron was discovered four years later.

stops.title: Nuclear Fission
stops.subtitle: Splitting the atom releases enormous energy
stops.description: When heavy nuclei split, the products weigh slightly less than the original — and that missing mass becomes energy via E = mc². The atomic age begins.

stops.title: Feynman and Quantum Electrodynamics
stops.subtitle: The most precise theory ever tested
stops.description: QED describes how light and matter interact with breathtaking precision — its predictions match experiments to 10 decimal places.

stops.title: The Big Bang
stops.subtitle: The universe had a beginning
stops.description: Galaxies are receding from us — faster the farther away they are. Run the movie backwards: everything began from a hot, dense singularity about 13.8 billion years ago.

stops.title: The Standard Model
stops.subtitle: The periodic table of fundamental particles
stops.description: The Standard Model catalogs all known fundamental particles and three of the four forces. It is the most successful theory in physics — and it is incomplete.

stops.title: The Higgs Boson
stops.subtitle: The particle that gives others mass
stops.description: The Higgs field permeates all of space. Particles that interact with it gain mass. The boson that proves the field's existence was detected at the LHC in 2012.

stops.title: Black Holes
stops.subtitle: Gravity so strong not even light can escape
stops.description: When mass is compressed past a critical density, spacetime curves so severely that nothing — not even light — can escape. We now have a photograph.

stops.title: Dark Matter
stops.subtitle: 85% of the matter in the universe is invisible
stops.description: Galaxies rotate too fast for the visible matter inside them to hold together. Something massive and invisible — dark matter — must be providing the extra gravity.

stops.title: Dark Energy
stops.subtitle: The universe's expansion is accelerating
stops.description: In 1998, astronomers found that distant supernovae were dimmer than expected — the universe isn't just expanding, it is expanding faster and faster. Something is pushing it apart.

stops.title: Gravitational Waves
stops.subtitle: Ripples in the fabric of spacetime, detected at last
stops.description: Two merging black holes a billion light-years away caused spacetime to ripple. LIGO stretched and squeezed a 4 km beam by one-thousandth the width of a proton.

stops.title: Quantum Computing
stops.subtitle: Using quantum mechanics to compute
stops.description: Classical bits are 0 or 1. Quantum bits (qubits) are both simultaneously — in superposition. Quantum computers use this to solve certain problems exponentially faster.

stops.title: The Open Questions
stops.subtitle: What physics still doesn't know
stops.description: What is dark matter made of? Why is there more matter than antimatter? How do gravity and quantum mechanics fit together? The greatest questions in physics remain open.
